title: Watch the First Spectacular Film Adaptation of the Odyssey (1911)
description:
in Film, History, Literature |   	July 9th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

PubôÙlic and comôÙmerôÙcial spaces around the world are now lined with imag
body:
in Film, History, Literature |   	July 9th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

PubôÙlic and comôÙmerôÙcial spaces around the world are now lined with imagery of a verôÙteôÙbra-studôÙded batôÙtle helôÙmet and statôÙues surôÙroundôÙed by flame. Itãs all part of the proôÙmoôÙtionôÙal camôÙpaign for ChristoôÙpher Nolanãs adapôÙtaôÙtion of theô Odyssey, which will begin openôÙing in theôÙaters latôÙer this month. Much has been said and writôÙten about how the project repôÙreôÙsentsô the next phase ofô Nolanãsô ever-grander cinôÙeôÙmatôÙic ambiôÙtions, butô bankôÙing on the specôÙtaôÙcle valôÙue of Homer has a long hisôÙtoôÙry in filmôÙmakôÙing. When the ItalôÙian silent adapôÙtaôÙtion LãOdisôÙsea came out in 1911, for examôÙple, it was uncerôÙtain even whether audiôÙences would tolôÙerôÙate the 44 minôÙutes it took to depict Odysseusã arduôÙous jourôÙney home.
Though it was released in the fall of 1911 in Italy and the folôÙlowôÙing winôÙter in the U.S.,ô LãOdisôÙsea now looks like a sumôÙmer blockôÙbusterô avant la letôÙtre, or ante litôÙterôÙamô ã or then again, givôÙen the mateôÙrôÙiôÙal,ô üüÃ§Ñö§ Ã§ö§ö¢ö¥öÝüö¡Ã¢ö§öÝö¿, though most of us are still waitôÙing to see just how ancient Nolan and his colôÙlabôÙoôÙraôÙtors have allowed themôÙselves to get.
By the stanôÙdards of their day, the makôÙers of LãOdisôÙsea appear to have spared no expense on sets, cosôÙtumes, and even visuôÙal effects, most notably in its porôÙtrayôÙal of the cyclops PolypheôÙmus. TechôÙniôÙcalôÙly, none of it may meaôÙsure up to what Nolan and comôÙpaôÙny have in store,ô but theô theôÙatriôÙcal gesôÙtures,ô shiftôÙing colôÙor tints, andô occaôÙsionôÙalôÙly batôÙtered texôÙtures do their part to conôÙjure up a realôÙiôÙty of their own.

LãOdisôÙseaô was actuôÙalôÙly the secôÙond major litôÙerôÙary adapôÙtaôÙtion of that year for its direcôÙtors, the trio of Francesco BertoliôÙni, AdolôÙfo Padovan, and Giuseppe De Liguoro, all workôÙing at the stuôÙdio Milano Films. Here on Open CulôÙture, weãve preôÙviôÙousôÙly feaôÙtured their first, LãInôÙferôÙno, which draôÙmaôÙtizes the first and most famous part of DanôÙteãsô Divine ComôÙeôÙdy at a length of 73 minôÙutes. That runôÙtime qualôÙiôÙfied it as the first feaôÙture-length film ever proôÙduced in Italy, by comôÙparôÙiôÙson to which LãOdisôÙseaô may have actuôÙalôÙly felt like a more familôÙiar viewôÙing expeôÙriôÙence to conôÙtemôÙpoôÙrary viewôÙersô accusôÙtomed to shorts. Now that humanôÙiôÙty has been re-accliôÙmatôÙed to watchôÙing things a few minôÙutes at a timeô here in the twenôÙty-twenôÙties, Nolanãs nearôÙly three-hour Odysseyô looks likeô a bold move indeed. But then, an epic poem demands an epic interôÙpreôÙtaôÙtion.
Note: If you click ãccã on the YouTube video above, EngôÙlish subôÙtiôÙtles will appear.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
Hear What Homerãs Odyssey SoundôÙed Like When Sung in the OrigôÙiôÙnal Ancient Greek
An InterôÙacôÙtive Map of Odysseusã 10-Year JourôÙney in Homerãs Odyssey
Hear the First Book of Homerãs IliôÙad Read Aloud in the OrigôÙiôÙnal Greek
Watch All 18,225 Lines of the IliôÙad Read by 66 Actors in a Marathon Event For an AudiôÙence of 50,000
Watch LãInferno (1911), Italyãs First FeaôÙture Film and PerôÙhaps the Finest AdapôÙtaôÙtion of Danteãs ClasôÙsic
CinecitôÙtû  Luce and Google to Bring Italyãs Largest Film Archive to YouTube
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: The Bayeux Tapestry Explained: Watch an Animated Retelling of the Norman Conquest
description:
in Art, History |   	July 10th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Every time the World Cup comes around, or at least since EngôÙland first and last won
body:
in Art, History |   	July 10th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Every time the World Cup comes around, or at least since EngôÙland first and last won it 60 years ago, thereãs talk of whether itãll be brought ãback home.ã The idea being, of course, that footôÙball (or socôÙcer, as itãs called in a couôÙple of the counôÙtries hostôÙing this yearãs matchôÙes) was made in EngôÙland. HowôÙevôÙer the showôÙdown with NorôÙway goes this SunôÙday, and indeed how the rest of the World Cup plays out durôÙing the week thereôÙafter, someôÙthing much oldôÙer ã and of much less debatôÙable oriôÙgins ã will be returned to Blighty: the Bayeux TapesôÙtry, which has been kept in the eponyôÙmous NorôÙmandy town since at least the fourôÙteen-sevôÙenôÙties, and most likeôÙly cenôÙturies earôÙliôÙer than that.
This sizôÙable and intriôÙcate piece of embroiôÙdered fabôÙric depicts the events leadôÙing up to the BatôÙtle of HastôÙings in 1066, the deciôÙsive event of the NorôÙman ConôÙquest of EngôÙland. LegôÙiôÙble today as a kind of ãmedieval comôÙic strip,ã as the narôÙraôÙtor of this new aniôÙmatôÙed video from the British MuseôÙum puts it, the Bayeux TapesôÙtry also reveals ãmedieval life in amazôÙing detail,ã while at the same time ãhintôÙing at secrets in its borôÙders.ã
For all the scholôÙarôÙly and popôÙuôÙlar attenôÙtion paid to it, the work has yet to yield the answers to anyôÙwhere near all of its mysôÙterôÙies, nor to lose its fasôÙciôÙnaôÙtion through familôÙiarôÙiôÙty. It bears, after all, quite a lot of imagery to get familôÙiar with in the first place.
Itãs one thing to behold the Bayeux TapesôÙtry through images, howôÙevôÙer high-resôÙoôÙluôÙtion, and quite anothôÙer to behold the real thing. The EngôÙlish have been able to get fairôÙly close to the latôÙter expeôÙriôÙence since the VicôÙtoôÙriôÙan era with the aid of the full-size repliôÙca, made in 1885, now disôÙplayed at the ReadôÙing MuseôÙum in BerkôÙshire and preôÙviôÙousôÙly feaôÙtured here on Open CulôÙture. But this SepôÙtemôÙber, the origôÙiôÙnal Bayeux TapesôÙtry will begin its resôÙiôÙdence at the British MuseôÙum, coinôÙcidôÙing with the renôÙoôÙvaôÙtion of the Bayeux MuseôÙum. (France, for its part, gets a loan ofô treaôÙsures from the ship buried at SutôÙton Hoo and the Lewis chessôÙmen.) If you get the opporôÙtuôÙniôÙty to have a look before itãs returned the folôÙlowôÙing year, donãt turn it down; as the World Cup shows us, you can nevôÙer be sure when the next homeôÙcomôÙing will hapôÙpen.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
The Bayeux TapesôÙtry Gets DigôÙiôÙtized: View the Medieval TapesôÙtry in High ResôÙoôÙluôÙtion, Down to the IndiôÙvidôÙual Thread
Behold a CreôÙative AniôÙmaôÙtion of the Bayeux TapesôÙtry
The StoôÙry Told on the Famous Bayeux TapesôÙtry Explained from Start to FinôÙish
The Entire HisôÙtoôÙry of the British Isles AniôÙmatôÙed: 42,000 BCE to Today
ConôÙstruct Your Own Bayeux TapesôÙtry with This Free Online App
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Albert Einstein Imposes on His First Wife a Cruel List of Marital Demands
description: Albert EinôÙstein pasôÙsionôÙateôÙly wooed his first wife MilôÙeôÙva MarôÙic, against his familyãs wishôÙes, and the two had a turôÙbuôÙlent but inte
body:
Albert EinôÙstein pasôÙsionôÙateôÙly wooed his first wife MilôÙeôÙva MarôÙic, against his familyãs wishôÙes, and the two had a turôÙbuôÙlent but intelôÙlecôÙtuôÙalôÙly rich relaôÙtionôÙship that they recordôÙed for posôÙterôÙiôÙty in their letôÙters. EinôÙstein and Maricãs love letôÙters have inspired the short film above, My LitôÙtle Witch (in SerôÙbian, I believe, with EngôÙlish subôÙtiôÙtles) and sevôÙerôÙal critôÙiôÙcal re-evalôÙuôÙaôÙtions of Einsteinãs life and MarôÙicãs influôÙence on his earôÙly thought. Some hisôÙtoôÙriôÙans have even sugôÙgestôÙed that Maricãwho was also trained in physicsãmade conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions to Einsteinãs earôÙly work, a claim hotôÙly disôÙputôÙed and, it seems, poorôÙly subôÙstanôÙtiôÙatôÙed.
The lettersãwritten between 1897 and 1903 and only disôÙcovôÙered in 1987ãreveal a wealth of preôÙviôÙousôÙly unknown detail about MarôÙic and the marôÙriage. While the conôÙtroôÙverôÙsy over MarôÙicãs influôÙence on EinôÙsteinãs theôÙoôÙries raged among acaôÙdôÙeôÙmics and viewôÙers of PBSãs conôÙtroôÙverôÙsial docôÙuôÙmenôÙtary, Einsteinãs Wife, a scanôÙdalous perôÙsonôÙal item in the letôÙters got muchô betôÙter press. As EinôÙstein and Maricãs relaôÙtionôÙship deteôÙriôÙoôÙratôÙed, and they attemptôÙed to scotch tape it togethôÙer for the sake of their chilôÙdren, the avunôÙcuôÙlar paciôÙfist wrote a chillôÙing list of ãconôÙdiôÙtions,ã in outôÙline form, that his wife must accept upon his return. Lists of Note tranôÙscribes them from WalôÙter Isaacsonãs biogôÙraôÙphy EinôÙstein: His Life and UniôÙverse:
CONDITIONS
A. You will make sure:
1. that my clothes and launôÙdry are kept in good order;
 2. that I will receive my three meals regôÙuôÙlarôÙly in my room;
 3. that my bedôÙroom and study are kept neat, and espeôÙcialôÙly that my desk is left for my use only.
B. You will renounce all perôÙsonôÙal relaôÙtions with me insoôÙfar as they are not comôÙpleteôÙly necôÙesôÙsary for social reaôÙsons. SpecifôÙiôÙcalôÙly, You will forego:
1. my sitôÙting at home with you;
 2. my going out or travôÙelôÙling with you.
C. You will obey the folôÙlowôÙing points in your relaôÙtions with me:
1. you will not expect any intiôÙmaôÙcy from me, nor will you reproach me in any way;
 2. you will stop talkôÙing to me if I request it;
 3. you will leave my bedôÙroom or study immeôÙdiôÙateôÙly withôÙout protest if I request it.
D. You will underôÙtake not to belitôÙtle me in front of our chilôÙdren, either through words or behavôÙior.
While it may be unfair to judge anyoneãs total charôÙacôÙter by its most glarôÙing defects, thereãs no way to read this withôÙout shudôÙderôÙing. Although EinôÙstein tried to preôÙserve the marôÙriage, once they sepôÙaôÙratôÙed for good, he did not lament MilôÙeôÙvaãs loss for long. ManôÙjit Kumar tells us in QuanôÙtum: EinôÙstein Bohr, and the Great Debate about the Nature of RealôÙiôÙty that although ãMilôÙeôÙva agreed to his demands and EinôÙstein returnedã
[I]t could not last. At the end of July, after just three months in Berlin, MilôÙeôÙva and the boys went back to Zurich. As he stood on the platôÙform wavôÙing goodôÙbye, EinôÙstein wept, if not for MilôÙeôÙva and the memôÙoôÙries of what had been, then for his two departôÙing sons. But withôÙin a matôÙter of weeks he was hapôÙpiôÙly enjoyôÙing livôÙing alone ãin my large apartôÙment in undiôÙminôÙished tranôÙquilôÙiôÙty.ã
EinôÙstein prized his soliôÙtude greatôÙly. AnothôÙer remark shows his difôÙfiôÙculôÙty with perôÙsonôÙal relaôÙtionôÙships. While he evenôÙtuôÙalôÙly fell in love with his cousin Elsa and finalôÙly divorced MarôÙic to marôÙry her in 1919, that marôÙriage too was trouôÙbled. Elsa died in 1936 soon after the couôÙple moved to the U.S. Not long after her death, EinôÙstein would write, ãI have gotôÙten used extremeôÙly well to life here. I live like a bear in my denãÎ This bearôÙishôÙness has been furôÙther enhanced by the death of my woman comôÙrade, who was betôÙter with othôÙer peoôÙple than I am.ã
Einsteinãs perôÙsonôÙal failôÙings might pass by withôÙout much comôÙment if he had not, like his hero GandôÙhi, been eleôÙvatôÙed to the staôÙtus of a ãsecôÙuôÙlar saint.ãô Yet, it is also the perôÙsonôÙal inconôÙsisôÙtenôÙcies, the weakôÙnessôÙes and petôÙty, even incredôÙiôÙbly calôÙlous moments, that make so many famous figôÙuresã lives comôÙpelling, if also conôÙfusôÙing. As EinôÙstein scholôÙar John Stachel says, ãToo much of an idol was made of EinôÙstein. Heãs not an idolãheãs a human, and thatãs much more interôÙestôÙing.ã
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2013.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
LisôÙten as Albert EinôÙstein Reads ãThe ComôÙmon LanôÙguage of SciôÙenceã (1941)
The MusiôÙcal Mind of Albert EinôÙstein: Great PhysiôÙcist, AmaôÙteur VioôÙlinôÙist and DevoôÙtee of Mozart
EinôÙstein DocôÙuôÙmenôÙtary Offers A RevealôÙing PorôÙtrait of the Great 20th CenôÙtuôÙry SciôÙenôÙtist
Albert EinôÙstein ExpressôÙes His AdmiôÙraôÙtion for MahatôÙma GandôÙhi, in LetôÙter and Audio
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.

title: International Aerial Photographer of the Year Contest Highlights the World from Above
description: For a structure that was completed nearly 90 years ago, San Franciscoãs Golden Gate Bridge possesses a kind of timeless modernity. Itãs been the s
body:
For a structure that was completed nearly 90 years ago, San Franciscoãs Golden Gate Bridge possesses a kind of timeless modernity. Itãs been the subject of countless photographs, often seen in the background from Baker Beach or from the overlook in Marin County. Its towers rise 500 feet from the roadway, but we typically can only see the structures from that level. For photographer Marcin Zajác, a droneãs-eye view revealed a unique perspective of this iconic landmark.
Zajácãs image is one of 101 finalists in the 2026 International Aerial Photographer of the Year, marking the second year of the competition. Photographers around the world submitted nearly 1,600 entries, with the top honor awarded to Azim Khan Ronnie, who draws on his background in journalism to capture human activities like fishing and harvesting crops.
Azim Khan Ronnie (Bangladesh), ãHarvesting Red Chiliã
Photographs considered for the competition range from drone views to shots taken from airplanes or the tops of buildings. Subject matter ranges from erupting volcanoes and geological phenomena to architecture and cultural events. See the top 101 photos on the contestãs website.
Vitaly Golovatyuk (Russia)
Paolo Lazzarotti (Italy), ãComplete Rainbowã
Paweé Jagieééo (Poland), ãThe Valley of Forgotten Riversã
Sanghamitra Sarkar (India), ãWariã
Avishek Patra (India), ãConfluenceã
Fabio Pappalettera (Italy), ãTumbling White Horseã
Kah-Wai Lin (U.S.), ãThunder of Hoovesã
A.J. Rich (U.S.), ãSerrationã
Clûˋment Coudeyre (Iceland), ãThe Floor Is Lavaã
Cûˋdric Tamani (Switzerland), ãDownstreamã
Luca Fucci (Italy), ãFlame Veinã

title: Rob Hann's Chromatic and Quirky Landscapes Evoke Long-Distance Road Trips
description: For more than three decades, Rob Hann has pursued the inimitable and notable, turning his lens toward public figures like Tom Hanks, David Byrne,
body:
For more than three decades, Rob Hann has pursued the inimitable and notable, turning his lens toward public figures like Tom Hanks, David Byrne, Chloe Sevigny, Ray Lotta, Willem Dafoe, and many others. He also ranges across the breadth and length of the U.S., traversing storied highways like U.S. 89 in Arizona, a popular route to the Grand Canyon, or U.S. 90 in Texas, which passes through the artistic enclave of Marfa. Not unlike the way he captures portraits of people, his characterizations of the countryãs endearingly quirky and remote places highlight individuality, presence, and the passage of time.
Hannãs subjects range from handmade road signs and vintage buildings to peculiar local attractions like trees completely covered in shoes. He sometimes captures site-specific artworks such as Nancy Holtãs ãSun Tunnelsã (1973-76) in Utahãs Great Basin Desert or Magda Sayegãs crocheted retro camper trailer at El Cosmico in Marfa. Thereãs often a tinge of tongue-in-cheek humor, too, such as a sign reading ãICYã amid an arid desert landscape in southeastern California or a half-buried car.
Clarksdale, Mississippi
ãAlthough my photos are usually very simple, I often like it when the viewer is not quite sure what it is that theyãre seeing, when they have questions,ã Hann tells Colossal. ãIãm often attracted to things that are amusing, unintentionally funny, or things that are puzzling.ã
For the vast majority of his career, Hann has shot with an analog Mamiya 7, only recently adding a digital camera to his routine: a Hasselblad X2D. One of the major differences is that the Mamiya takes a roll of film with only 10 frames, so thereãs a more disciplined approach to avoid wasting rolls. With digital cameras, the number of images is only limited by the space on an SD card. ãI try to make good decisions as I shoot and avoid wading through huge amounts of images on my computer when I get home,ã Hann says. ãWith both cameras, I only use one fixed lens, and when shooting film, I just use one film stock.ã
Color images have also emerged as an important tenet of Hannãs practice despite an earlier preference for black-and-white photography. ãI was required to also shoot colour when working for magazines and record companies,ã he says. ãI could do it but struggled to make the colour photos special or make them feel like my own. It took me many years to get my head around making consistently good colour images.ã Today, the emotive potential of color, explored through different levels of saturation and warmth, plays a significant role.
Hann is currently working with The Artist Edition toward the publication of a new book titled Wonder Valley, which is slated for release sometime later this year. Follow updates on Instagram.
Lucerne Valley, California
Highway 62, California
Grand Forks, North Dakota
Yucca Valley, California
Marfa, Texas
Mojave, California
Highway 287, Montana
Needles, California
Mojave, California
Highway 89, Arizona

title: Ana Elisa Egreja Takes a Magical Realist Approach to Migration in Her Rich Still Lifes
description: ãImprobable but not impossibleã is how Brazilian artist Ana Elisa Egreja describes the unexpected companions in her vibrant still lifes. Combining
body:
ãImprobable but not impossibleã is how Brazilian artist Ana Elisa Egreja describes the unexpected companions in her vibrant still lifes. Combining the architectural motifs, animals, and fare common in her native SûÈo Paulo with elements from abroad, Egreja positions domestic spaces as sites of change, where migration and cross-cultural pollination come to bear.
In a new suite of 15 oil paintings, the artist draws on the long tradition of Dutch Golden Age still lifes alongside the contrived qualities of collage. Tablescapes filled with fresh flowers and shiny produce also contain cellophane-wrapped snacks and canned goods. Egreja acknowledges flight as a rich symbol of freedom and migration, and birds swirl overhead and perch atop the uncanny objects. Thereãs also a pair of window pieces, blanketed in 24-karat gold leaf and decorative wrought grilles, which serve as an interstitial spot for the winged creatures to pause as they move between interior and exterior. 
ãInterior with Five Cats at Sunset [Interior com Cinco Gatos ao PûÇr do Sol]ã (2026), oil on canvas with beaded curtain, 63 x 74 3/4 inches
Egrejaãs focus on bridging these divides emerges in her renditions of sunsets, too, with their bold gradients rippling from crimson to amber across living spaces. This glowing feature backdrops both ãInterior with a Jaguar and Sun Conure,ã in which a forlorn feline lounges on an Art Deco sofa, and ãInterior with Five Cats at Sunset.ã The latter also contains a sculptural element as the vibrant light streams through a beaded curtain mounted to the paintingãs edge.
Taking a magical realist approach to migration, Egreja questions the hard boundaries we perceive between private and public space, wildness and domesticity, as well as international borders. She also renders these lines illegible to our non-human counterparts, nodding to an ongoing organic exchange between seemingly disparate entities.
The works shown here are part of the artistãs first solo exhibition in the U.S., titled The Flight of Color, which runs from July 16 to September 5 at Jessica Silverman in San Francisco. Explore more of the artistãs practice on Instagram.
ãInterior with a Jaguar and Sun Conure [Interior com OnûÏa-Pintada e Jandaias-Sol]ã (2026), oil on canvas, 63 x 74 3/4 inches
ãWindow with Scarlet Tanagers and a Golden Sky [Janela com Tiûˆs-Sangue e Cûˋu de Ouro]ã (2026), oil and 24 karat gold leaf on canvas, 31 1/2 x 31 1/2 inches
ãRed Table with Chinoiserie, Macaws, and Parrots [Mesa Vermelha com Chinoiserie, Araras, e Papagaios]ã (2026), oil and 24 karat gold leaf on canvas, 47 1/4 x 86 5/8 inches
ãStill Life with Embroidered Fabric [Natureza Morta com Tecido Laranja]ã (2026), oil and fabric on canvas, 11 3/4 x 15 3/4 inches
ãWindow with Parrots and a Golden Sky [Janela com Papagaios e Cûˋu de Ouro]ã (2026), oil and 24-karat gold leaf on canvas, 31 1/2 x 47 1/4 inches
ãMagenta Still Life (Sunset) [Natureza Morta Magenta]ã (2026), oil and 24-karat gold leaf on canvas, 14 1/8 x 23 5/8 inches

title: Atmospheric Scenes and Layered Interpretations Top the 2026 Hasselblad Masters Contest
description: In Pioche, Nevada, a movie theater built in 1937 stands in tribute to the immense changes in technology and style over nearly a century. A bold po
body:
In Pioche, Nevada, a movie theater built in 1937 stands in tribute to the immense changes in technology and style over nearly a century. A bold portrait set against a black background by photographer Kevin Boyle captures not only the aging building but a mid-20th-century pastime that taps into nostalgia and small town identity. (Amid preservation efforts, the theater is currently being rehabilitated.) Itãs just one of the works in the artistãs ongoing Movieland series, which won the Architecture category in this yearãs Hasselblad Masters 2026 contest.
Additional categories in the prestigious competition include Wildlife, Landscape, Art, Portrait, Street, and Project//21, which highlights work by entrants aged 21 or younger. This year, photographers in 160 countries submitted more than 108,000 images, which were narrowed down to 70 finalists.
Svetlana Jovanovic, from ãOthernessã
In addition to their technical aptitude, the artists illuminate lesser-known pockets of the world by examining distinctive social issues, nature, and geographies. The winning photos are atmospheric and even surreal, such as Rohan Reillyãs low-exposure Ephemeral Visions series of rows of trees and Gosse Boumaãs misty depictions of Netherlandish market stalls in Morning Ritual.
Svetlana Jovanovic turns her lens to bold dual portraits of identical twins, and Alfred Minnaarãs otherworldly captures of underwater vitality topped the Wildlife category. Panitbhand Paribatra Na Ayudhya, winner of the Project//21 category, is a 14-year-old scuba diver from Thailand who explores the beauty, fragility, and biodiversity of the ocean in beautiful portrayals of marine life.
Hasselblad Foundation executive director Kalle Sanner says, ãAcross categories, the strongest work operated on more than one level simultaneously: legible on first encounter, yet resistant to easy interpretation. These are images that require attention, that continue to unfold the longer you stay with them.ã For example, Yudha Kusuma Puteraãs uncanny photographs of huddled cowsã backs nod to the unseen. What we donãt see is as significant as what we do, as these animals are actually grazing on heaps of trash in a dump on the outskirts of Yogyakarta, Indonesia. Exploring notions of labor and visibility, Putera takes a visually literal approach to ãout of sight, out of mind.ã
See all the winning images on the Hasselblad Masters website.
